text: IN THE COURT OF APPEAL OF THE DEMOCRATIC SOCIALIST
REPUBLIC OF SRI LANKA
In the matter of an application for Restitution, in the nature of Restitutio-In-Integrum under and in terms of Article 138 of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka.

Court of Appeal Case No: RII/0009/2025 Warnakulasuriya Sineth Eranda Lowe, Seda Watta, DC Puttalam Mundalama Case No: 6166/D Plaintiff VS Dona Anne Niroshani Hettiarachchi, Temple Road, Madurankuliya Defendant AND NOW BETWEEN Dona Ann Niroshani Hettiarachchi, Correct Address No. 603/B, New Road, Wennappuwa And Present 508/268 Flinders Street, Melbourne, VIC, 3000, Australia Represented by Power of Attorney Holder Warnakulasuriya Marie Anne Jenita Fernando of No. 603/B. New Road, Wennappuwa Defendant Petitioner

Vs. Warnakulasuriya Sineth Eranda Lowe, Seda Watta, Mundalama And Correct Address No. 175 Pahalawatta, Lunuwila, Wennappuwa And Present 17-19 Mile Street, Mulgrave Melbourne Australia Plaintiff-Respondent Brianna Fiori Lowe, No. 175, Pahalawatta, Lunuwila, Wennappuwa 1st-Respondent The Registrar, District Court of Puttalam, Puttalam 2nd -Respondent The Registrar, Registrar of Marriage, Denzil Kobbekaduwa Mawatha Battaramulla 3rd -Respondent Before : R. Gurusinghe, J. & Dr. S. Premachandra, J.

Counsel : Nishan Premathiratna, AAL with Ms. Shenali Dias, AAL and Ms. Nimashi Fernando, AAL Instructed by Demean Law Chambers for the Respondent Shiral Lankathilaka, AAL Instructed by N.J.P. Silva AAL for the Petitioner Supported on: 28-01-2026 Decided on : 19-03-2026

ORDER R. Gurusinghe, J. The plaintiff-respondent (the respondent) and defendant-petitioner (the petitioner) were legally married husband and wife. The respondent filed a divorce action against the petitioner in the District Court of Puttalam and obtained a divorce decree. Petitioner in this application alleged that the respondent obtained the above divorce decree while the petitioner was in Australia, without serving summons to the petitioner, giving a wrong address to the District Court and also filing a forged proxy purported to be of the petitioner. The petitioner in this application seeks to set aside the divorce decree and the order for custody of a minor daughter. On behalf of the respondent the following preliminary objections were taken up.
a) There is no merit supporting the affidavit before Court. As the affidavit
dated 04-03-2025 was by the power of attorney holder, who could not have had any personal knowledge of the application.
b) The petitioner failed to prefer the per incuram application to the original
Court and wilfully by passed the Appellate remedy. No valid grounds for a restitutio-in-integrum application.

The respondent argued that the affidavit dated 04-03-2025 is mere hearsay and a verbatim copy of the petition, where the Power-of-Attorney holder had no involvement in the original proceedings before the District Court of Puttalam, in case no. 6166/D. On behalf of the respondent, it further argued

that according to the provisions of section 181 of the Civil Procedure Code, the affidavit shall be confined to statements of such facts as the declarant is able, of his own knowledge and observation, to testify to, except on interlocutory applications. The petitioner has filed documents marked P1 to P12 (xv). P9 is a certified copy of the District Court of Puttalam, Case No. 6166. Most of the facts pleaded are reflected in that case record.

On behalf of the petitioner, it was argued that the affidavit is legally authorised, that the Court of Appeal has discretion to correct injustice caused to the petitioner through its revisionary powers, and that fraud invalidates all proceedings. In the case of Kumarasinghe vs. Ratnakumara and others [1983] 2 SRI LR 393, the Supreme Court inter alia stated as follows:

While a stranger cannot make an affidavit, it need not be made by the party individually, but may be made by any person who is personally aware of the facts. The court is entitled to have the best evidence before it; where there exists evidence which is first-hand it will be most unsatisfactory to place before court evidence of any other description. Ordinarily a petitioner is the best person who can speak to the facts and verify the facts averred in the petition; then, it is he who should file the affidavit in support of the said facts; but if there are other witnesses too who can, to their personal knowledge, depose to those facts there is no bar to their filing affidavits in support of the petition, in addition to or in substitution for the petitioner's affidavit. But if the petitioner does not file his own affidavit verifying the facts, which he is personally conversant with, then the court would be extremely reluctant to grant relief. But the petitioner may be excused from filing an affidavit, if for some good reason or ground he is unable to do so.

The Addl. Solicitor-General's objection postulates our reading into rule 65(1) (a) words which are not there. The rule says that the petitioner shall support his petition by an affidavit. It does not predicate that the person swearing the affidavit must be the petitioner himself.

When perusing the petition, affidavit, and the documents filed by the petitioner, Court sees there are sufficient grounds to proceed with the application rather than to decide on technical issues, especially when there is an alleged fraud. The documents show that the petitioner was not in Sri Lanka at the time of the divorce action. If the petitioner has appointed an

Attorney-at-Law to appear before the Court, it should have been done through a Power-of-Attorney holder. The case record does not show that a Power-of-Attorney was filed of record or at least referred to in the answer. If the summons was not served on the petitioner in the divorce action as pleaded by the petitioner, all proceedings against the petitioner become a nullity. In the case of Ittepana vs. Hemawathie [1981] 1 Sri LR 476, Justice Sharvananda (as he then was) stated at Page 484 as follows:

Failure to serve summons is a failure which goes to the root of the jurisdiction of the Court to hear and determine the action against the defendant. It is only by service of summons on the defendant that the Court gets jurisdiction over the defendant. If a defendant is not served with summons or is otherwise notified of the proceedings against him, judgment entered against him in those circumstances is a nullity, and when the Court is made aware of this defect in its jurisdiction, the question of rescinding or otherwise altering the judgment by the Court does not arise since the judgment concerned is a nullity.

When the proceedings become a nullity, it is a nullity for all intents and purposes. In the case of Wathukarage Shantha Merril Kumara vs. National Gem and Jewellery authority and Others SC/MISL/04/2004, the Supreme Court, quoting an English judgment, stated as follows:

If a decision is ultra vires, it is a nullity for all intents and purposes. It is void, not voidable. Everything that stems from a decision which is a nullity also automatically becomes a nullity without further ado. Lord Denning in Macfoy v. United Africa Co. Ltd. [1961] 3 All ER 1169 at 1172 articulates this notion in his own inimitable style in this way:

If an act is void, then it is, in law, a nullity. It is not only bad, but incurably bad. There is no need for an order of the court to set it aside. It is automatically null and void without more ado, though it is sometimes convenient to have the court declare it to be so. And every proceeding which is founded on it is also bad and incurably bad. You cannot put something on nothing and expect it to stay there. It will collapse.
This passage was referred to with approval by Justice G.P.S. de Silva (as he then was) in Rajakulendran v. Wijesundera [1982] 1

Sri Kantha LR 164 at 168-169, Justice Sharvananda (as he then was) in Sirisena v. Kobbekaduwa, Minister of Agriculture and Lands (1978) 80 NLR 1 at 182, and Justice Sripavan (as he then was) in Leelawathie v. Commissioner of National Housing [2004] 3 Sri LR 175 at 178 and in many more judgments. If the proceedings are a nullity, this court cannot allow them to be perpetuated relying on mere technical grounds. The preliminary objections are overruled. The application will be fixed for hearing.
Judge of the Court of Appeal. Dr. S. Premachandra J. I agree. Judge of the Court of Appeal.
